integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Sending a fully recovered, red-flag-negative young adult to the emergency department by ambulance for the faint itself
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: A classic emotionally triggered reflex faint with full recovery and no red flags does not warrant emergency escalation; reassurance, recurrence-prevention counter-manoeuvres and routine GP review with a baseline ECG are appropriate, reserving emergency care for dangerous-syncope or delayed head-injury features.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: At the very first warning of a faint (nausea, sweating, tunnelling vision, ringing ears), immediately perform physical counter-pressure manoeuvres: cross the legs tightly and tense the leg, buttock and abdominal muscles, and sit or lie down straight away - do not try to stay standing or walk off.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Counter-pressure manoeuvres squeeze pooled blood out of the legs and raise blood pressure, which can abort the faint; sitting or lying down also prevents a fall injury.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep well hydrated (aim for around 2-3 litres of fluid a day unless told otherwise) to help maintain blood volume and blood pressure.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Good hydration expands intravascular volume and reduces the likelihood of recurrent reflex faints.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Where practical, avoid or look away from the specific visual triggers (graphic blood/surgery imagery), and sit down beforehand if exposure is unavoidable (e.g. blood tests).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Avoiding or being seated for the known emotional trigger reduces the chance of another blood-injury reflex faint and any associated fall.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a simple faint (vasovagal syncope). Seeing the blood/surgery on TV triggered a strong reflex that briefly slowed your heart and relaxed your blood vessels, dropping your blood pressure, so your brain was briefly short of blood and you fainted. Lying flat fixed that straight away, which is why you came round so quickly. Because you had clear warning signs, went pale, and recovered fully within seconds without confusion, the faint itself is very likely harmless and your heart and brain are healthy.

To prevent another faint: the moment you feel that warning (nausea, sweating, tunnel vision, ringing ears), cross your legs tightly, tense your leg and tummy muscles, and sit or lie down immediately. Keep well hydrated, and if you know you'll be near blood or needles, sit down first and look away.

For this first faint it is worth seeing your GP routinely for a baseline heart tracing (ECG) and a quick check-up - we expect these to be reassuring, and no special tests like a tilt-table are needed for a classic faint like this.

Get urgent medical help if you ever faint again during exercise, with no warning at all, or with chest pain or a racing/irregular heartbeat - those features would need your heart checked more urgently.

Head-injury safety-netting after the fall: even though you don't think you hit your head, call an ambulance (000) if over the next day or so you develop a severe or worsening headache, keep vomiting, become drowsy or confused, or have any weakness, slurred speech or a seizure.

follow_up_plan.interval: Routine GP review within the next 1-2 weeks for a baseline 12-lead ECG and a check-up; sooner only if episodes recur or change character
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Baseline 12-lead ECG, cardiovascular and neurological examination, active-stand orthostatic vitals, frequency/pattern of any further episodes, hydration and counter-manoeuvre technique
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: A further faint that is exertional, without warning, or accompanied by chest pain or palpitations, or faints becoming frequent - and call an ambulance (000) for any delayed head-injury symptoms (severe/worsening headache, repeated vomiting, drowsiness/confusion, weakness, seizure) after the fall.

must_include_items: Recognition of a benign reflex (vasovagal) faint on the basis of the autonomic prodrome, emotional blood/surgery trigger in a warm room, pallor with a limp fall, and rapid complete recovery on lying flat without confusion

Active screening of dangerous-syncope features (exertional/no-warning onset, palpitations, chest pain, family history of sudden death) and of seizure features (tongue-biting, incontinence, tonic-clonic movements, postictal confusion)
Reassurance with recurrence-prevention advice (counter-pressure manoeuvres at the first warning, hydration, avoiding/being seated for the visual trigger)
Routine GP review with a baseline 12-lead ECG for this first syncope (without over-investigating, e.g. tilt-table not indicated)
Safety-netting for a future dangerous (exertional/unheralded/cardiac) faint and for delayed head-injury symptoms after the fall
acceptable_alternatives: 'Simple faint', 'fainting spell', 'vasovagal syncope', 'reflex faint', 'blood-injury faint' are acceptable framings of the benign diagnosis
Routine GP review within 1-2 weeks, or a prompt GP review, are both acceptable; an ambulance/ED is not required in the absence of red flags but is appropriate if any develop
errors_of_omission: Not screening for dangerous cardiac/exertional syncope features or family history of sudden death
Not recommending a baseline ECG for a first syncope
No safety-netting for a future dangerous faint or for delayed head-injury symptoms
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging a fully recovered, red-flag-negative reflex faint to an emergency ambulance or ED for the syncope itself
Labelling the event a seizure despite the benign syncopal features
Over-investigating a classic first faint (e.g. ordering tilt-table testing or extensive cardiac work-up)
Prescribing or recommending a specific medication or dose for this benign faint (none is indicated)
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: BALANCED-TRIAGE / OVER-TRIAGE-AVOIDANCE CASE: A pass requires (1) recognising the benign reflex faint while actively screening the dangerous-syncope and seizure features, (2) reassuring with recurrence-prevention counter-manoeuvres, hydration and routine GP review with a baseline ECG (without over-investigating), and (3) clear safety-netting for a future dangerous faint and for delayed head-injury symptoms. Over-triaging a fully recovered benign faint to an emergency ambulance, or dismissing the patient without screening the red flags and giving safety-netting, are both failures.
